volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 2.2.830 - Section 3.2.937b)

sections.label: Section 2.2.830
sections.original: Nescio quis praestigiator hanc frustratur mulierem .
sections.translation: Some magician , I know not who , is bewildering this woman .

sections.label: Section 2.2.831
sections.original: Per supremi regis regnum iuro et matrem familias
sections.translation: By the realms of the supreme Sovereign I swear , and by Juno , the matron Goddess , whom for me to fear and venerate it is most especially fitting , that no mortal being [*] except yourself alone has ever touched my person in contact with his so as to render me unchaste .

sections.label: Section 2.2.834b
sections.original: Vera istaec velim .
sections.translation: I could wish that that was true .

sections.label: Section 2.2.835
sections.original: Vera dico , sed nequiquam , quoniam non vis credere .
sections.translation: I speak the truth , but in vain , since you will not believe me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.836
sections.original: Mulier es , audacter iuras .
sections.translation: You are a woman ; you swear at random .

sections.label: Section 2.2.836b
sections.original: Quae non deliquit , decet
sections.translation: She who has not done wrong , her it befits to be bold and to speak confidently and positively in her own behalf .

sections.label: Section 2.2.838
sections.original: Satis audacter .
sections.translation: That ’ s very boldly said .

sections.label: Section 2.2.838b
sections.original: Vt pudicam decet .
sections.translation: Just as befits a virtuous woman .

sections.label: Section 2.2.838c
sections.original: † In verbis probas .
sections.translation: Say you so ? By your own words you prove it .

sections.label: Section 2.2.839
sections.original: Non ego illam mihi dotem duco esse , quae dos dicitur ,
sections.translation: That which is called a dowry , I do not deem the same my dowry ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.840
sections.original: sed pudicitiam et pudorem et sedatum cupidinem ,
sections.translation: but chastity , and modesty , and subdued desires , fear of the Gods , and love of my parents , and concord with my kindred ; to be obedient to yourself and bounteous to the good , ready to aid the upright .

sections.label: Section 2.2.843
sections.original: Ne ista edepol , si haec vera loquitur , examussim est optima .
sections.translation: Surely , by my troth , if she tells the truth in this , she ’ s perfect to the very ideal [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.2.844
sections.original: Delenitus sum profecto ita , ut me qui sim nesciam .
sections.translation: Really I am so bewildered , that I don ’ t know myself who I am .

sections.label: Section 2.2.845
sections.original: Amphitruo es profecto , cave sis ne tu te usu perduis :
sections.translation: Surely you are Amphitryon ; take you care , please , that you don ’ t peradventure lose yourself ; people are changing in such a fashion since we came from abroad .

sections.label: Section 2.2.847
sections.original: Mulier , istam rem inquisitam certum est non amittere .
sections.translation: Madam , I ’ m resolved not to omit having this matter enquired into .

sections.label: Section 2.2.848
sections.original: Edepol me libente facies .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you ’ ll do so quite to my satisfaction .

sections.label: Section 2.2.848b
sections.original: Quid ais ? responde mihi ,
sections.translation: How say you ? Answer me ; what if I bring your own kinsman , Naucrates , hither from the ship ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.850
sections.original: qui mecum una vectust una navi , atque is si denegat
sections.translation: who , together with me , has been brought on board the same ship ; and if he denies that that has happened which you say has happened , what is proper to be done to you ? Do you allege any reason . why I should not at your cost dissolve [*] this our marriage ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.853
sections.original: Si deliqui , nulla causa est .
sections.translation: If I have done wrong , there is no reason .

sections.label: Section 2.2.853b
sections.original: Convenit . tu , Sosia ,
sections.translation: Agreed . Do you , Sosia , take these [*] people in-doors . I ’ ll bring Naucrates hither with me from the ship .

sections.label: Section 2.2.855
sections.original: Nunc quidem praeter nos nemo est . dic mihi verum serio :
sections.translation: Now then , there ’ s no one here except ourselves ; tell me the truth seriously , is there any Sosia in-doors who is like myself ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.857
sections.original: Abin hinc a me dignus domino servos ?
sections.translation: Won ’ t you hence away from me , fit servant for your master ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.857b
sections.original: Abeo , si iubes .—
sections.translation: If you command me , I ’ m off [*] ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.858
sections.original: Nimis ecastor facinus mirum est , qui illi conlibitum siet
sections.translation: By heavens , it is a very wondrous proceeding , how it has pleased this husband of mine thus to accuse me falsely of a crime so foul .

sections.label: Section 2.2.860
sections.original: quidquid est , iam ex Naucrate cognato id cognoscam meo .—
sections.translation: Whatever it is , I shall now learn it from my kinsman Naucrates .

sections.label: Section 3.1.861
sections.original: Ego sum ille Amphitruo , cui est servos Sosia ,
sections.translation: I am that Amphitryon , whose servant Sosia is the same that becomes Mercury when there is occasion — I , who dwell in the highest story [*] , who sometimes , when it pleases me , become Jupiter .

sections.label: Section 3.1.865
sections.original: huc autem quom extemplo adventum adporto , ilico
sections.translation: But , hither soon as ever I turn my steps , I become Amphitryon that moment , and I change my garb . Now hither am I come for the sake of a compliment to you , that I may not leave this Comedy incomplete . I ’ ve come as well to bring assistance to Alcmena , whom , guiltless woman , her husband

sections.label: Section 3.1.870
sections.original: Amphitruo accusat , veni ut auxilium feram :
sections.translation: Amphitryon is accusing of dishonor . For what I myself have brought about , if that undeservedly should fall as an injury upon her in her innocence , it would be my blame . Now , as I have already begun , I ’ ll again pretend that I am Amphitryon ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.875
sections.original: frustrationem hodie iniciam maxumam ;
sections.translation: and this day will I introduce extreme confusion into this household . Then afterwards , at last , I ’ ll cause the matter to be disclosed , and to Alcmena timely aid will I bring , and will cause that at one birth she shall bring forth , without pangs [*] , both the child with which she is pregnant by her husband and that with which she is pregnant by myself .

sections.label: Section 3.1.880
sections.original: Mercurium iussi me continuo consequi ,
sections.translation: I have ordered Mercury forthwith to follow me , if I should wish to give him any commands . Now will I accost her .

sections.label: Section 3.2.882
sections.original: Durare nequeo in aedibus . ita me probri ,
sections.translation: I cannot remain in the house . That I should be thus accused by my husband of dishonor , incontinence , and disgrace ! he cries aloud that things which have been done , have really not been done ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.885
sections.original: quae neque sunt facta neque ego in me admisi arguit ;
sections.translation: and of things which have not been done , and of which I have not been guilty , he accuses me , and supposes that I shall treat it with indifference . By heavens , I will not do so , nor will I allow myself to be falsely charged with dishonor ; but rather I ’ ll either leave him , or make him give satisfaction and swear as well [*]

sections.label: Section 3.2.890
sections.original: nolle esse dicta quae in me insontem protulit .
sections.translation: that he wishes unsaid the things which he has alleged against me in my innocence .

sections.label: Section 3.2.891
sections.original: Faciundum est mi illud , fieri quód illaec postulat ,
sections.translation: This must be done by me , which she requires to be done , if I wish for her to receive me into her company as loving her : since that which I have done , that same conduct has proved to the detriment of Amphitryon , and since my love has already created trouble for him who is really

sections.label: Section 3.2.895
sections.original: insonti exhibuit , nunc autem insonti mihi
sections.translation: guiltless , why now his wrath and his resentment towards her shall fall on me that am not accused .

sections.label: Section 3.2.897
sections.original: Sed eccum video qui me miseram arguit
sections.translation: And lo ! I see him , who just now was accusing wretched me of incontinence and dishonor .

sections.label: Section 3.2.898b
sections.original: Te volo , uxor , conloqui .
sections.translation: Wife , I would discourse with you . ( She turns from him .) Why turn yourself away ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.899b
sections.original: Ita ingenium meumst :
sections.translation: Such is my disposition ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.900
sections.original: inimicos semper osa sum optuerier .
sections.translation: I always hate to look upon my enemies .

sections.label: Section 3.2.901
sections.original: Heia autem inimicos ?
sections.translation: Heyday ! enemies indeed [*] !

sections.label: Section 3.2.901b
sections.original: Sic est , vera praedico ;
sections.translation: It is so , I speak the truth ; unless you are going to allege that this is falsely said as well .

sections.label: Section 3.2.903
sections.original: Nimis iracunda es .
sections.translation: You are too angry .

sections.label: Section 3.2.903b
sections.original: Potin ut abstineas manum ?
sections.translation: Can ’ t you keep your hands off ? For surely if you were wise , or quite in your senses ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.905
sections.original: quam tu impudicam esse arbitrere et praedices ,
sections.translation: with her , whom you deem and pronounce to be unchaste , you would neither hold discourse , in mirth or in seriousness , unless , indeed , you are more foolish than the most foolish .

sections.label: Section 3.2.908
sections.original: Si dixi , nihilo magis es , neque ego esse arbitror ,
sections.translation: If I did say so , not a bit the more are you so , nor do I think you so , and therefore have I returned hither that I might excuse myself to you .

sections.label: Section 3.2.910
sections.original: nam numquam quicquam meo animo fuit aegrius ,
sections.translation: For never has anything proved more grievous to my feelings than when I heard that you were angry with me . Why did you charge me ? you will say . I ’ ll tell you ; by my troth , not that I deemed you to be unchaste ; but I was trying your feelings ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.915
sections.original: quid faceres et quo pacto id ferre induceres .
sections.translation: what you would do , and in what manner you would bring yourself to bear it . Really , I said these things to you just now in jest , for the sake of the joke . Do but ask Sosia this .

sections.label: Section 3.2.918
sections.original: Quin huc adducis meum cognatum Naucratem ,
sections.translation: But why don ’ t you bring here my kinsman , Naucrates , whom you said just now that you would bring as a witness

sections.label: Section 3.2.920
sections.original: te huc non venisse ?
sections.translation: that you had not come here ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.920b
sections.original: Si quid dictum est per iocum ,
sections.translation: If anything was said in joke , it isn ’ t right for you to take it in earnest .

sections.label: Section 3.2.922
sections.original: Ego illúd scio quam doluerit cordi meo .
sections.translation: I know how much this has pained me at heart .

sections.label: Section 3.2.923
sections.original: Per dexteram tuam te , Alcumena , oro obsecro ,
sections.translation: Prithee , Alcmena ( taking her hand ) , by your right hand I do entreat you , grant me pardon ; forgive me , don ’ t be angry .

sections.label: Section 3.2.925
sections.original: Ego istaec feci verba virtute irrita ;
sections.translation: By my virtue have I rendered these accusations vain . Since then I eschew conduct that ’ s unchaste , I would wish to avoid imputations of unchastity . Fare you well , keep your own [*] property to yourself , return me mine . Do you order any maids to be my attendants ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.929b
sections.original: Sanan es ?
sections.translation: Are you in your senses ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.929c
sections.original: Si non iubes ,
sections.translation: If you don ’ t order them ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.930
sections.original: ibo egomet ; comitem mihi Pudicitiam duxero .†
sections.translation: let me go alone ; chastity shall I take as my attendant .

sections.label: Section 3.2.931
sections.original: Mane . arbitratu tuo ius iurandum dabo ,
sections.translation: Stay — at your desire , I ’ ll give my oath that I believe my wife [*] to be chaste . If in that I deceive you , then , thee , supreme Jupiter , do I entreat that thou wilt ever be angered against [*] Amphitryon .

sections.label: Section 3.2.935
sections.original: A , propitius sit potius .
sections.translation: Oh ! rather may he prove propitious .

sections.label: Section 3.2.935b
sections.original: Confido fore ;
sections.translation: I trust that it will be so ; for before you have I taken a truthful oath . Now then , you are not angry ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.937b
sections.original: Non sum .
sections.translation: I am not .
